title: The Mid-Life What Do I Actually Believe
subtitle: A reading on the question that surfaces in the late thirties and early forties, when the inherited beliefs no longer fit
excerpt: A twenty-minute reading on the question that arrives in the late thirties and early forties: what do I actually believe, now that the inherited beliefs no longer fit. The answer is rarely the one you were raised with, and rarely a clean replacement.
sourceNotes: Original to homemade.education. The mid-life faith inventory is shared across writing on midlife development, on faith development (James Fowler, Stages of Faith, 1981), and on the writing of people who have lived through the shift.

practice.whenToUse: Read when the inherited beliefs no longer fit and a clean replacement has not arrived. Also good in the year of a big life change, when the question gets louder.

content.text: Somewhere in the late thirties or early forties, the question turns up. What do I actually believe. It is not the same question you were asked at sixteen. The answers from then are not the answers you would write now.

The question is often a quiet one. A line in a book. A talk with a friend who has just been to a funeral. A Sunday morning when the old routine no longer holds. The question is asking, even if you have not sat with it yet.
Why the question turns up now

By midlife, you have lived enough to see how the early beliefs held up. The promises did not all come true. The rules turned out to be more flexible than they were sold. Some of the inherited shape no longer fits the life you actually have.

The faith-development writer James Fowler called this stage individuative-reflective faith. The work of the stage is to pick up the inherited shape and look at it. Take the parts that still hold. Set down the parts that do not.

The question is not a vote. You do not have to pick a side. You do not have to choose religion or no religion, spirit or no spirit. The question is about what is true for you, on the inside, that you can carry through the rest of your life.
It is also not a one-day question. The answer comes slowly. The point is the asking, not the closing of the case.
Write the question down. Sit with it for ten minutes a week, for a few months.
Read widely. One book from inside the tradition you came from. One book from outside. One book from a tradition you know nothing about.
Talk with one or two people who can hold the question with you. Not the ones who already know the answer they want you to land on.
Let the body have a vote. The body votes on what is true with a yes-feeling or a no-feeling, even when the head is still arguing.
Be patient. The clean answer rarely arrives. The shape of what you believe will grow over years.
Where the answer lives in the meantime

While you wait for the shape of it, the answer lives in the small acts. How you treat the people in front of you. What you do with your time. How you spend your money. What you stand for when it costs you something. The belief is in the days, not the doctrine.

Original to homemade.education. The midlife faith inventory is shared across writing on midlife. It draws on James Fowler's Stages of Faith from 1981. It also draws on the writing of people who have lived through the shift.
